does: This paper proposes Instruction Continual Pre-training (InsCP), a method for adapting English-focused large language models to new target languages while preserving their instruction-following and safety capabilities. The core innovation integrates instruction tags directly into the continual pre-training phase rather than treating pre-training and instruction fine-tuning as separate sequential stages. The authors demonstrate that InsCP achieves language transfer while maintaining conversational proficiency, RLHF alignment, and harmful content filtering—using only 0.1 billion tokens of high-quality instruction data. Evaluations across language alignment, reliability, and knowledge benchmarks confirm that the approach successfully transfers models to target languages without the performance degradation typically observed with standard continual pre-training followed by supervised fine-tuning.
connects: This work addresses a well-established challenge in multilingual NLP: the English-centric bias of large language models and the difficulty of efficiently adapting them to new languages. It builds on prior research in continual pre-training for domain and language adaptation, as well as decades of work on supervised fine-tuning for instruction following. The paper directly responds to known problems with sequential CP-then-SFT pipelines, which prior work has shown degrades safety and alignment properties. The approach integrates insights from instruction tuning literature with continual learning paradigms, positioning it as an evolution of existing practices like those used in models such as Llama and other multilingual adaptations that face similar trade-offs between linguistic capability and instruction adherence.
enables: This approach enables more efficient multilingual LLM deployment by reducing the computational and data requirements for language adaptation. By demonstrating that instruction integration during pre-training preserves safety and alignment properties, the work opens pathways for rapid expansion of instruction-following models across diverse languages with minimal resource overhead. The reduced token requirement (0.1B tokens) makes language transfer more accessible to researchers and organizations with limited computational budgets. Future work can build on this method to extend coverage to underrepresented languages, explore whether the approach generalizes across model architectures and sizes, and investigate whether similar instruction-integration strategies could preserve other model properties during domain adaptation or catastrophic forgetting scenarios.
